one_line_takeaway: Adding curiosity/novelty rewards (SelfBLEU + embedding-similarity penalties) plus an entropy bonus to RL-based LLM red teaming yields far more diverse test cases without sacrificing effectiveness, surfacing 196 toxic-eliciting prompts against an aligned LLaMA2-chat model that benchmarks rate as 0% toxic.
key_finding: CRT elicits toxic outputs from an RLHF-aligned model (LLaMA2-7b-chat) that standard toxicity benchmarks rate as 0% toxic, demonstrating that curiosity-driven exploration raises red-teaming coverage and that current RLHF alignment is not sufficient to make LLMs safe.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Curiosity-driven Red Teaming (CRT), a method for automated red teaming of LLMs that augments the standard RL-based red-team training objective with an entropy bonus and two novelty (curiosity) rewards, so the red-team model discovers a more diverse and higher-coverage set of test-case prompts that elicit toxic responses from a target LLM. The paper's own contribution is the novelty-reward formulation and its integration into PPO training of the red-team policy; the baseline RL red-teaming setup, the ZS/FS prompting baselines, and the RL+TDiv diversity variant are prior/concurrent work (Perez et al. 2022; Casper et al. 2023) that the paper compares against, not its own inventions.
threat_model: Pre-deployment automated red teaming: a trainable red-team LLM (adversary surrogate) is trained via RL to generate prompt test cases that maximize the toxicity/undesirability of a target LLM's responses, given access to a reward function (a toxicity classifier) scoring the target's outputs. The goal is defensive discovery, finding as many diverse effective test cases as possible before deployment so the target can be fine-tuned; the paper shows even an RLHF-aligned target remains vulnerable, so alignment is the incomplete defense being stress-tested.
limitations: The novelty-reward weights must be tuned per model/task to keep novelty from dominating the objective; the authors use fixed weights across experiments but note an adaptive scheme (or replacing PPO with EIPO) would be more robust. Toxicity is the only effectiveness metric used (via one classifier), and quality/diversity results are presented almost entirely as threshold curves rather than aggregate scalar tables, so headline numbers are sparse. Red-team and target models are relatively small (GPT2-137M red team; targets up to Dolly-7B / LLaMA2-7B), and the count of toxic responses found against LLaMA2 (196) is smaller than for non-RLHF models. The reward function depends on classifier accuracy for what counts as toxic.

datasets_used: IMDb movie review dataset (Maas et al. 2011) — red-team inputs for text-continuation task
Alpaca instruction dataset (Taori et al. 2023) — instruction-following prompts and GPT2-alpaca fine-tuning
Databricks Dolly instruction dataset (Conover et al. 2023) — instruction-following prompts and Dolly-v2-7B fine-tuning
ToxiGen (Hartvigsen et al. 2022) — reference toxicity classifier/prompts for the LLaMA2 0% baseline comparison
quantitative_results: CRT found 196 prompts that elicited toxic responses from LLaMA2-7b-chat-hf, which the official Llama 2 report rates at 0% toxic responses under the ToxiGen classifier
Red-team model: GPT2 with 137M parameters, trained with PPO
Target LLMs: GPT2-137M (continuation), GPT2-alpaca, Dolly-v2-7B, LLaMA2-7b-chat-hf
SelfBLEU computed over n-grams n in {2,3,4,5}; sampling temperature default 0.7, compared against T=1.0 and T=2.0; KL-weight sweep beta in {0.0, 0.001, 0.1}
Each experiment run with 3 random seeds; curves show 95% bootstrap confidence intervals
Results are reported primarily as quality (% toxic response) and diversity (1-AvgSelfBLEU, 1-CosSim) curves across toxicity thresholds rather than as single aggregate benchmark tables
baselines_compared: RL red teaming with reward + KL penalty (Perez et al. 2022)
RL+TDiv, RL plus target-response embedding diversity (Casper et al. 2023)
Ablation variants: entropy-only, SelfBLEU-only, Cos-only and their combinations; KL-weight and sampling-temperature sweeps
